text: Thank you very much for joining on another abdominal wall surgery video. Today we're going to be talking about ETAP ventral hernia repair.
This is one of the most common approaches used in advanced abdominal wall surgery. It was a seventy-two year old man presenting with an incisional hernia following robotic prostatectomy.
The extraction site is located in the midline, revealing a hernia measuring approximately six by six centimeters.

These types of hernias can be approached in a variety of ways. I opted to do an ETEP approach as I felt a preperitoneal approach or intraperitoneal would not be in the best interest of the patient or the outcome of the surgery.
We planned for a left retromuscular lateral doc, which is likely the most common approach for ETEP for most people.
The surgeon begins laparoscopic access using a 5mm Applied Medical KII Fios trocar, facilitating air insufflation during trocar placement.
Upon entry, the surgeon uses the camera to perform dissection, achieving a view that facilitates the placement of robotic trocars on the lateral side.

The surgeon incises approximately one centimeter away from the linea alba, penetrating through the preperitoneal fat layer to identify the contralateral posterior rectus sheath in the retromuscular space.
The surgeon enters the contralateral retrorectus space, a key step in the ETEP approach, revealing the anatomical structures within this area.
It really has helped facilitate extraperitoneal surgery for more advanced hernias.
The surgeon dissects within the contralateral space, developing a clear plane. A second trocar is visible on the opposite side, indicating intentional placement for the procedure.
With my trainees, I'll oftentimes have them gain access to one of the retro-rectus spaces with a five millimeter trocar to get comfortable with ETEP access.
The surgeon works down the midline, reducing the preperitoneal fat and preparing to address the herniated contents. The visual field primarily shows diastasis at this stage.
The surgeon identifies the superior margin of the hernia, with visible small bowel protruding into the hernia sac.
The surgeon reduces the hernia, revealing bowel that has fallen through. The hernia sac is now visible, allowing for more aggressive use of electrocautery to dissect the sac.

The surgeon pushes the hernia sac away, ensuring the ipsilateral posterior rectus sheath is isolated from the viscera and hernia sac. Energy is used to leave a cuff, simplifying the anterior fascial closure.
The surgeon creates an opening in the hernia sac, revealing a hole to check for any adhesions within the sac.
The surgeon performs preperitoneal and retromuscular dissection, identifying multifocal defects and progressing down to the retropubic space, known as the space of Retzius.

The surgeon observes a distance of at least fifteen centimeters between the inferior elements of the hernia and the pubis, indicating no need for myopectenial orifice dissection. The procedure progresses to anterior fascial closure.

The surgeon performs a posterior closure of a small defect in the hernia using an O permanent V-lock suture. The anterior fascia is then closed with sequential tightening of the suture, gradually bringing the tissue defects together.

The surgeon pulls the suture along the same vector as the incision to facilitate smooth passage. The diastasis is being plicated, with the hernia visible to the left. The plication is reversed, aiding in the closure of the defect, with additional plication performed below the hernia.

The surgeon uses a number one permanent V-lock suture to close the defect, with the defects visibly coming together. The diastasis has already been plicated below, and the surgeon runs multiple sutures back over themselves to lock them in place.

The surgeon places a 24 by 30 centimeter mesh, unrolling it like a carpet from inferior to superior within the surgical field. Fiber and sealant are applied to secure the mesh, and no retro muscular drain is used.
